properties.name: Adventure Pass, Explained
properties.meta_title: Adventure Pass: What It Is, What It Costs & Where You Need It (2026)
properties.meta_description: The Southern California Adventure Pass in two minutes: $5/day or $30/year, required to park at most developed Forest Service trailheads in the Angeles, Cleveland, Los Padres and San Bernardino forests, covered by America the Beautiful passes, and yes — rangers ticket.
properties.card_summary: $5/day or $30/year to park at most developed Forest Service trailheads. The two-minute version.
properties.quick_answer: If you're parking at a developed Forest Service trailhead in Southern California, assume yes. The Adventure Pass costs $5 per day or $30 per year and hangs from your mirror or sits on the dash. An America the Beautiful interagency pass covers it. There's usually no booth — it's honor-system signage, and rangers do write tickets.
properties.where_needed: Developed fee sites in the Angeles, Cleveland, Los Padres and San Bernardino national forests — most maintained trailhead lots
properties.where_not: National parks (Zion, Bryce, Arches); state, county and city parks (Torrey Pines, Lake Poway); private land (Bowen Ranch's $10/person is separate); non-fee roadside areas; driving through without stopping
properties.enforcement: Honor-system signage; rangers ticket at popular lots

sections.heading: The two-minute version
sections.body: The Adventure Pass is the parking fee system for four Southern California national forests — the Angeles, Cleveland, Los Padres and San Bernardino. Where it applies — developed fee sites, which in practice means most maintained trailhead lots in those forests — you display a pass in your parked car: $5 for a day pass, $30 for the annual, sold at ranger stations and local vendors (many mountain gas stations and general stores carry them). If you already have an America the Beautiful interagency pass (the $80 national parks annual), you're covered — hang it and go. That's usually the better buy for anyone who visits both parks and forests.

sections.heading: Where people get burned
sections.body: Three recurring mistakes. First, assuming no booth means no fee — Adventure Pass sites are honor-system signage with real ranger enforcement, and the popular lots (Heart Rock, Heaps Peak) get checked. Second, assuming the pass covers everything — private land is separate: Bowen Ranch's $10/person fee at Deep Creek has nothing to do with the Forest Service. Third, buying a day pass at the trailhead — most fee lots have no on-site sales, so buy before you drive up. Each trailhead guide on this site says whether the pass applies, so you don't have to guess. Fourth, and increasingly common as people range further afield: assuming it works outside the national forests. It doesn't — and that includes national parks, which are a different agency entirely: at Zion or Arches you pay a park entrance fee, not an Adventure Pass. Torrey Pines is California State Parks, Lake Poway is a city park, and neither honors an Adventure Pass — they run their own fees. Nor does the pass have anything to do with a permit: the free Cucamonga Wilderness permit at Icehouse Canyon and the $6 Recreation.gov permit at Cedar Creek Falls are separate from whatever goes on your dash.

faq.q: How much is an Adventure Pass?
faq.a: $5 for a day pass, $30 for an annual. An America the Beautiful interagency pass ($80/year) also covers Adventure Pass sites.

faq.q: Where do I buy an Adventure Pass?
faq.a: Ranger stations and local vendors — many mountain-town gas stations and general stores sell them. Most trailhead lots have no on-site sales, so buy one before driving to the trailhead.

faq.q: Do rangers actually check for the pass?
faq.a: Yes, especially at popular lots like Heart Rock and Heaps Peak. The sites are honor-system signage backed by ranger citations.

faq.q: Does the Adventure Pass cover Deep Creek Hot Springs?
faq.a: Not on the Bowen Ranch side — that's private property with its own $10/person cash fee. The free Bradford Ridge roadside off Hwy 173 isn't a designated fee site either.

properties.name: About TrailheadParking.com
properties.meta_title: About TrailheadParking.com: Who We Are & How Facts Get Verified
properties.meta_description: TrailheadParking.com covers the parking situation at trailheads across Southern California and the Utah national parks — lot sizes, fill times, passes, shuttles, closures — with every fact checked against official sources and date-stamped. From the makers of BigBearMap.com and LakeArrowheadMap.com.
properties.card_summary: Who runs this site, how facts get verified, and the sister guides it grew out of.

sections.heading: What this site is
sections.body:
TrailheadParking.com does one thing: it tells you what parking is actually like at mountain trailheads — how many cars fit, when the lot fills, what pass you need, what enforcement looks like, and what the realistic plan B is. It grew out of a pattern we kept seeing while building our area guides: the number-one thing that ruins a mountain day isn't the trail, it's arriving at 10am to a full lot with no plan.

Coverage spans four Southern California ranges — the San Bernardinos (Lake Arrowhead–Crestline–Running Springs, Big Bear, Forest Falls), the San Gabriels (Chantry Flat, Switzer Falls, East Fork, Icehouse Canyon), the San Jacintos (Idyllwild) and San Diego County (Cedar Creek Falls, Three Sisters Falls, Mt. Woodson, Torrey Pines) — plus Zion, Bryce Canyon and Arches, where shuttles, permit lotteries and reservation systems replace the usual question of lot size. We go where the parking question is genuinely confusing and the official answer is genuinely findable — the Park Service publishes lot-by-lot capacities that most forests simply don't.

sections.heading: How facts get verified
sections.body: Every fee, lot description, and access rule is checked against the official source — the Forest Service, the county, the operator — at the time the page is written, and every page carries a facts last verified date at the bottom. Mountain rules change seasonally, so perishable facts get re-checked on a recurring pass. When we get something wrong, we fix the page rather than defend it. No business pays to appear here and no placement is sponsored. Where something is genuinely uncertain — like the informal pullouts at the Arrowhead Pinnacles — the page says so plainly instead of faking precision.

sections.heading: The family of sites
sections.body: This site comes from the same maker as BigBearMap.com and LakeArrowheadMap.com — practical, verification-first guides to the San Bernardino mountain communities. The trailhead pages here link to those guides for the trails themselves; this site stays focused on the lot.
